planting.note: Sow 4–6 weeks before last frost date; Telephone is a tall climber reaching 5–6 feet and requires sturdy support — grow on strong netting or trellis strung between tall posts.

directions: Sow seed 1–2 inches deep and 2–3 inches apart in cool, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun, 4–6 weeks before last frost; Telephone pea (sold in Britain as Alderman) is the classic tall heirloom main-crop shell pea, a vigorous climber to 5–6 feet that produces large, curved dark-green pods with 8–10 sweet, tender peas per pod — significantly more productive per plant than bush varieties and the pod size that made it famous in Victorian kitchen gardens. Requires strong netting or pea sticks for support. Harvest when pods are well-filled and peas are young and sweet; productivity drops rapidly if pods are left to mature fully on the vine.

planting.note: Sow in early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost; a tall climber to 4–5 feet requiring netting or trellis support; harvest young pods before seeds begin to swell for best eating quality.

directions: Sow seed 1–2 inches deep in cool, fertile soil in full sun 4–6 weeks before last frost, providing 4–5 feet of netting or trellis support; Mammoth Melting Sugar is the most widely grown heirloom snow pea, an heirloom variety documented since at least the early 1900s producing large (4–6 inch), flat, tender pods with a delicate, melt-in-the-mouth texture when harvested young and cooked lightly in stir-fries or eaten raw. The pods are stringless when young and the standard against which other snow peas are judged for tenderness and flavor. Harvest when pods are 3–4 inches long and absolutely flat with no seed bulge visible for the premium eating experience.

common_name: Dwarf Grey Sugar Pea (Snow Pea, Grey Sugar)

planting.note: One of the earliest snow peas to mature; sow very early in spring (8 weeks before last frost) for a quick cool-season harvest before summer heat arrives; compact habit needs only minimal support (24–30 in twiggy branches or short netting).

directions: Sow seed 1–2 inches deep in early spring (up to 8 weeks before last frost) in cool, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Dwarf Grey Sugar is an old heirloom snow pea, documented since at least the 1800s, distinguished by its unusually attractive purple-tinged vines and flowers against blue-grey foliage — the purplish cast coming from anthocyanins that also make the young tendrils and pods slightly reddish before the pods mature to green. Compact habit (24–30 inches) makes it ideal for small gardens and containers. An early and productive snow pea; harvest pods frequently when flat and young before seeds swell.

planting.note: Sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; a tall climber (4–5 ft) with attractive purple flowers requiring trellis support; pods change from bright yellow-green to full yellow at harvest — pick when flat and yellow-green.

directions: Sow seed 1–2 inches deep in cool, fertile soil in full sun 4–6 weeks before last frost with trellis support; Golden Sweet is a distinctive heirloom snow pea from India with vivid lemon-yellow pods against deep green foliage and attractive purple flowers — one of the most ornamental edible peas available. The flavor of the bright yellow flat pods is sweet and delicate with a slightly more tender texture than green snow peas; they make a visually striking addition to salads and stir-fries. Harvest pods when fully yellow and flat before seeds begin to swell for best quality. The yellow color provides an interesting contrast to green and purple-podded pea varieties grown together.

planting.note: Direct sow 4–8 weeks before last frost; Lincoln (also sold as Homesteader, the same variety under a different name) is one of the most highly regarded American shelling peas for flavor — a mid-season bush pea with wrinkled seeds and large, well-filled pods that many home gardeners consider the finest-flavored of all commonly available shelling peas.

directions: Sow 1–1.5 inches deep, 2–4 inches apart in rows 18–24 inches apart; direct seed as soon as soil can be worked in early spring. Lincoln (sold as Homesteader in Britain and by some American companies — both names refer to the same variety) is a mid-season American shelling pea considered by many gardeners and seed savers to produce the finest overall flavor and texture among commonly available bush peas; the compact plants produce blunt-tipped pods with 7–9 large, wrinkled (sweeter) peas each, ripening in 65–70 days from sowing. The wrinkled seed type indicates higher sugar content compared to smooth-seeded varieties; like all garden peas, Lincoln must be harvested at precise peak ripeness and eaten promptly before sugars convert to starch. An excellent variety for freezing as well as fresh eating; it remains the benchmark for shelling pea flavor among serious home gardeners.

planting.note: Direct sow 4–8 weeks before last frost; Little Marvel is a 1934 All-America Selections winner — a remarkably compact dwarf bush pea (15 inches tall) that produces an outsized crop relative to its tiny size, making it ideal for small-space gardens, raised beds, containers, and intensive succession planting.

directions: Sow 1–1.5 inches deep; Little Marvel can be planted in raised beds at 15-inch row spacing due to its dwarf habit and needs only minimal support. Little Marvel pea won an All-America Selections award in 1934 and remains one of the most productive and most compact garden shelling peas available — the dwarf 15-inch plants are among the shortest of all shelling peas while producing an outsized crop of well-filled pods with 6–8 wrinkled, sweet peas each. The compact habit makes Little Marvel excellent for small raised beds, succession planting in containers, and tight-spaced intensive gardens where full-sized pea varieties would be impractical. Does not require staking even in wind, though a small 12–18 inch support fence improves harvestability. The 60–65 day maturity is relatively early for a sweet wrinkled-seeded pea.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–6 weeks before last frost in spring or in fall 8–10 weeks before first frost; Wando is the most heat-tolerant commonly available shelling pea, developed by USDA for Southern gardens — it sets pods in warmer conditions that cause bloom drop in all other pea varieties, making it the recommended choice for Gulf Coast and Southern spring pea planting.

directions: Sow 1–1.5 inches deep 2–6 weeks before last frost; Wando tolerates both cool and moderately warm conditions and is particularly valuable for later-season spring planting in Southern zones. Wando pea was developed by USDA and named for the Wando River in the Charleston, South Carolina area, reflecting its adaptation to warm Southern conditions. Unlike most shelling peas that drop flowers and fail to set pods when temperatures rise above 75°F, Wando continues producing pods at temperatures 5–10°F warmer than other varieties, extending the harvest window in Gulf Coast and Southern gardens. The 24–30 inch plants produce smooth-seeded peas (less sweet than wrinkled but better adapted to warm-soil planting) in pods with 6–8 peas; the flavor is good. Exceptionally valuable for fall pea production in zones 7–10 when planted in late August–September.
sources: Virginia Cooperative Extension Vegetable Production Guide

planting.note: Direct sow 4–8 weeks before last frost; Oregon Sugar Pod is a 1971 All-America Selections winner snow pea with large, flat, edible pods and resistance to pea enation mosaic virus — one of the most popular and disease-resistant snow peas available, excellent for both Pacific Northwest and Southern gardens.

directions: Sow 1–1.5 inches deep in rows 18–24 inches apart; provide a 2–3 foot trellis or fence for support. Oregon Sugar Pod pea won the All-America Selections award in 1971 and remains one of the most widely grown and most reliable snow peas, selected for both outstanding pod quality and resistance to pea enation mosaic virus (PEMV), a significant disease problem in humid growing regions. The semi-dwarf plants produce large (4–4.5 inch), flat, very sweet pods harvested when the pod wall is still translucent and peas inside are just barely beginning to swell — the 'snow pea' harvest window when the entire flat, stringless pod is eaten. Used in stir-frying, sautéing, and raw in salads, where the flat pods retain their crisp texture and bright color well when cooked quickly over high heat.

planting.note: Direct sow 6–10 weeks before last frost — one of the earliest peas available, germinating in soils as cold as 38°F; Early Alaska is the traditional extra-early pea for late winter and earliest spring planting, maturing before all other commonly grown varieties and finishing before summer heat arrives — ideal for short-season northern gardens and earliest-possible spring pea harvests in the South.

directions: Sow 1–1.5 inches deep as soon as soil can be worked in early spring; in zone 6+ this means January–February; in zone 3–5, as soon as soil thaws. Early Alaska is one of the oldest and most cold-tolerant pea varieties, germinating in soils as cold as 38–40°F and maturing in 55–60 days from seed — making it one of the fastest peas available. The smooth-seeded peas are not as sweet as wrinkled-seed types but have traditional pea flavor and historically represent the 'early pea' of American kitchen gardens. Short (24–30 inch), self-supporting plants produce cylindrical pods with 5–7 peas; smooth seeds tolerate cold, wet soil better than wrinkled types that can rot. In Gulf Coast and Southern gardens (zones 8–10), Early Alaska planted in January finishes before March heat ends the pea season.
